class_schedule.topic: Beyond Figma: The Advanced Prototyping Landscape
learning_objectives: Evaluate prototyping tools by their capabilities and limitations
Select the right tool for the interaction fidelity required
Understand when design-tool prototypes are insufficient and code is needed
readings: Prototyping: A Practitioner's Guide by Todd Zaki Warfel, Ch. 5-8

lecture_notes.introduction: Figma prototyping has limits. Advanced prototyping reaches into code-based tools, hardware integration, and multi-device experiences that cannot be simulated in a design tool alone.
key_concepts: Prototyping tool landscape: Figma, Framer, ProtoPie, Origami Studio, code
Capability mapping: which interactions each tool can simulate
Code-based prototyping: HTML/CSS/JS for realistic web interactions
The prototype fidelity decision matrix: effort vs. learning value
Multi-device prototyping: interactions that span phone, watch, and speaker
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building the same interaction — a drag-to-reorder list — in Figma (limitations visible), ProtoPie (more realistic), and HTML/JS (fully functional), comparing effort and output
setup_requirements: Code editor (VS Code) for HTML/JS prototyping

review.value: Choose by what you need to test: Figma for click-through flows, ProtoPie for sensor-based and multi-device interactions, Framer for realistic web interactions, code for anything needing real data or APIs
A prototype tests a hypothesis and is disposable. An MVP is a shippable product with minimal features. Never confuse the two — prototypes should be cheap to throw away.

class_schedule.topic: Code-Based Prototyping with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
learning_objectives: Build interactive prototypes using HTML, CSS, and vanilla JavaScript
Implement realistic form interactions, animations, and data manipulation
Use browser APIs for prototyping: geolocation, camera, local storage
readings: HTML & CSS: Design and Build Websites by Jon Duckett, Ch. on Forms and Animations

lecture_notes.introduction: Code-based prototypes bridge the gap between design mockups and real products. They use real data, respond to real inputs, and feel like actual software — invaluable for testing complex interactions.
key_concepts: HTML/CSS prototyping: semantic markup for realistic form interactions
CSS animations and transitions for motion prototyping
JavaScript event handling: click, input, scroll, drag events
Browser APIs: geolocation, camera, local storage for realistic prototypes
Rapid code prototyping: using frameworks like CodePen, Replit, or Glitch
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a functional search-with-filter prototype in HTML/JS that fetches and displays real data from a public API
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension
CodePen or Replit account for rapid prototyping
Basic understanding of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript

review.value: Code prototypes can use real data, real APIs, and real browser features — making user testing more ecologically valid than testing with placeholder content and simulated interactions
Use CodePen or Replit for speed — no build setup, instant preview, shareable URL for remote testing. Perfection is not the goal; testability is.

learning_objectives: Prototype interactions using device sensors: accelerometer, gyroscope, proximity
Design and test multi-device experiences spanning phone, tablet, and watch
Use ProtoPie for sensor-driven and cross-device prototyping
readings: ProtoPie documentation: Sensor interactions and Bridge features
Designing Connected Products by Rowland et al., Ch. 4-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern UX extends beyond touch. Devices have accelerometers, gyroscopes, cameras, proximity sensors, and voice input. Advanced prototyping must simulate these multi-modal interactions.
key_concepts: Sensor triggers: tilt, shake, proximity, sound level, compass
Multi-device flows: phone triggers action on TV, watch receives notification
ProtoPie Bridge: connecting multiple prototypes across devices in real time
Voice interaction prototyping: simulating conversational UI
AR prototyping: using camera feed as interaction context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a multi-device prototype in ProtoPie: a phone app that sends a notification to a watch prototype, which then triggers a response on a tablet dashboard
setup_requirements: Two or more physical devices for cross-device testing

review.value: ProtoPie can respond to device tilt (accelerometer), shake, proximity (face near phone), compass orientation, and sound level — enabling prototype testing of motion and context-aware interactions
ProtoPie Bridge connects prototypes running on different devices — enabling realistic testing of experiences that span phone, watch, tablet, and computer simultaneously

learning_objectives: Connect prototypes to real or mock APIs for dynamic data display
Use Framer to build prototypes with real data fetching and display
Prototype personalization and dynamic content without engineering support
readings: Framer documentation: Data and CMS features
Designing with Data by Rochelle King, Ch. 2-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Prototypes with placeholder content test layout and flow. Prototypes with real data test whether the design actually works when content varies in length, density, and meaning.
key_concepts: Framer's CMS and data fetching capabilities for realistic prototypes
Mock API services: json-server, Mockoon, or Airtable as data sources
Content stress testing: what happens when titles are 3 words vs. 30?
Dynamic content: prototyping personalization, recommendations, and feeds
Edge case data: empty results, very long strings, missing images, special characters
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a product listing prototype in Framer that fetches real product data from a mock API, displays it dynamically, and handles loading, empty, and error states
setup_requirements: Sample data set with edge cases

review.value: Testing designs with extreme content: very long titles, very short descriptions, missing images, special characters, right-to-left text. If the design only works with ideal placeholder content, it is not production-ready.
Tools that simulate real API behavior without backend development — json-server creates a full REST API from a JSON file in seconds, enabling data-driven prototypes for user testing

learning_objectives: Match prototype complexity to the research question being tested
Conduct A/B prototype tests to compare design alternatives
Build a prototype testing strategy into the product development process
readings: Sprint by Jake Knapp, Ch. on Prototyping and Testing
Lean UX by Jeff Gothelf, Ch. on Experiment Design

lecture_notes.introduction: The purpose of every prototype is to answer a question. Advanced prototyping means choosing the minimum viable prototype that answers the question — not the most impressive one you can build.
key_concepts: Question-driven prototype selection: what fidelity answers this question?
A/B prototype testing: building two alternatives and testing which performs better
Prototype metrics: task success, time-on-task, preference, comprehension
The prototype lifecycle: build, test, learn, discard or iterate
Integrating prototyping into agile sprints: design sprint methodology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running an A/B prototype test with the class: two different checkout flow prototypes, measuring task success rate, time-on-task, and preference across both versions
setup_requirements: Two completed prototypes for A/B testing
Maze or UserTesting for remote unmoderated testing

review.value: The simplest prototype that can answer your specific research question — a paper prototype for navigation testing, a coded prototype for performance perception, a sensor prototype for gesture discovery

Testing two design alternatives (not just one) generates comparative data that is more actionable than absolute data — 'Version B had 40% faster task completion than Version A' is more compelling than 'Users completed the task in 45 seconds'

questions.question: A team needs to prototype a mobile app feature where users shake their phone to undo the last action, and the app responds with a haptic pulse and an undo animation. Explain why Figma cannot prototype this and recommend an alternative tool with justification.
questions.answer: Figma cannot prototype this because: (1) Figma has no access to device sensors (accelerometer/gyroscope) needed to detect the shake gesture. (2) Figma cannot trigger haptic feedback on physical devices. (3) While Figma can simulate the visual undo animation, it cannot connect it to a physical shake trigger. Recommended tool: ProtoPie, because it supports accelerometer sensor triggers (shake detection), can trigger device haptic responses, supports complex conditional animations, and runs on real mobile devices. The prototype would use a shake trigger to initiate the undo animation and a haptic response simultaneously, testing the full interaction on a real device.

rubric.key: Recommends ProtoPie or equivalent with correct reasoning
Describes how the alternative tool solves each limitation

questions.question: When should a UX designer build a code-based prototype instead of using a design tool like Figma? List three scenarios and explain the advantage of code in each.
questions.answer: Three scenarios: (1) Testing with real data — when you need to see how designs handle actual content variations (long titles, missing images, dynamic lists). Code can fetch from APIs; Figma uses static content. (2) Testing complex form interactions — multi-step forms with real validation, conditional fields, and autocomplete. Code handles input, validation logic, and state persistence that Figma cannot. (3) Testing performance perception — when you need users to experience real loading times, network delays, and rendering behavior. Code runs in a real browser; Figma prototypes load instantly. In each case, code provides ecological validity — the prototype behaves like the real product, so test findings are more reliable.

rubric.key: Three valid scenarios with clear context
Code advantage explained for each scenario
Mentions ecological validity or realism benefit

questions.question: You built a beautiful prototype with 'Lorem Ipsum' placeholder text and ideal-length product names. During user testing, everything tested well. Why might these results be misleading, and how would you improve the prototype?
questions.answer: The results are misleading because placeholder content masks design problems that real content would expose: (1) Lorem Ipsum has even character distribution — real text may have very long words that break layouts. (2) Ideal-length product names hide how the design handles 3-word vs. 30-word titles, missing descriptions, or very long price strings. (3) Users cannot evaluate information comprehension with meaningless placeholder text — they cannot judge whether the hierarchy helps them find what they need. Improvement: Replace placeholders with real or realistic content, including edge cases — the shortest and longest possible titles, missing images (fallback state), products with and without reviews, prices in different currencies. Test with a range of realistic content to validate that the design system handles reality.

rubric.key: Identifies content length/variation as hidden problem
Explains comprehension testing limitation of Lorem Ipsum
Proposes specific content stress testing improvements

questions.question: Explain the concept of a 'minimum viable prototype' and give an example of choosing a lower-fidelity prototype over a higher-fidelity one that would actually yield better research results.
questions.answer: A minimum viable prototype is the simplest prototype that can answer the specific research question — not the most impressive or realistic prototype possible. Example: You need to test whether users understand a new navigation structure before investing in visual design. A higher-fidelity interactive Figma prototype with full visual design would actually be worse because users would comment on colors, fonts, and imagery instead of focusing on navigation findability. A lower-fidelity tree test (text-only hierarchy) isolates the navigation question by removing all visual design variables. The tree test yields cleaner data about information architecture effectiveness. The principle: prototype fidelity should match and isolate the variable you are testing. Higher fidelity introduces confounding variables.

rubric.key: Correct definition of minimum viable prototype
Example shows lower-fidelity yielding better data
Explains confounding variables from excess fidelity

major_assignments.title: Advanced Prototype Showcase
major_assignments.description: Build three prototypes of the same feature using three different tools: Figma, a code-based approach (HTML/JS or Framer), and ProtoPie. Compare the fidelity, effort, and user testing results across all three. Deliver a comparative analysis and recommendation.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency in multiple prototyping tools and approaches
Evaluate tool trade-offs through direct comparison on the same feature
Test each prototype version with users and compare data quality
Develop a prototyping tool selection framework
deliverables: Feature specification document describing the interaction being prototyped
Figma prototype with maximum achievable fidelity for the chosen interaction
Code-based prototype (HTML/JS or Framer) with real data integration
ProtoPie prototype with sensor or multi-device interaction
Comparative analysis report: effort, fidelity, test results, recommendations

criteria: Pushes Figma to its limits for the chosen interaction
Uses advanced features: variables, conditionals, smart animate
Clearly documents where Figma falls short
Polished and testable
Functional with real or mock data integration
Interaction feels native and responsive
Code is reasonably clean and maintainable
Demonstrates clear advantage over Figma version
Uses sensor or multi-device capabilities not available in other tools
Interaction is testable on real devices
Takes advantage of ProtoPie's unique capabilities
Demonstrates clear use case for the tool
Effort tracked and compared honestly across all three
User test data compared with analysis of data quality differences
Tool selection framework is generalizable beyond this project
Recommendations are evidence-based and nuanced
Three distinct prototypes demonstrating proficiency across tools
Meaningful comparative analysis with real user testing data
Clear tool selection framework with evidence-based recommendations
Professional documentation of process and findings

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on prototyping tools, techniques, and selection criteria

tool_labs.description: Weekly labs building prototypes in different tools with increasing complexity

advanced_prototype_showcase.description: Three-tool prototype comparison project with user testing and analysis

prototype_critiques.description: Peer critique of prototypes evaluating fidelity, interaction quality, and testability

final_portfolio.description: Portfolio showcasing prototyping skills across tools and fidelity levels

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory as they involve hands-on tool instruction. Missing labs means missing tool demonstrations that are not recorded. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Labs build sequentially on tool skills. Late labs penalized 10% per day. The final showcase has a firm deadline with 15% daily penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer critique and tool troubleshooting encouraged. All prototypes must be individual work. Sharing code prototypes or ProtoPie files between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All prototypes must be original work. Using tutorial code or ProtoPie templates without significant modification and attribution is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma, Framer, ProtoPie (all free tiers available), VS Code, a mobile device for sensor-based prototyping, and optionally a secondary device for multi-device testing.
